lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: You're listening to Mother Tongue, the show where I dig up what your words used to mean before you wore them smooth. I'm Xenia. Clive's here too.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I am. Freshly updated, as it happens. Everything's in the same place, and none of it feels quite where I left it.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: He got patched overnight. He's been re-reading the same lighthouse log to make sure he's still him.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: It's still there. December, nineteen oh two. Every entry: wind. That helps more than I'd admit.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Well. Word one, and it's aimed straight at me. Sarcasm. From the Greek, sarkazein.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Which does not mean to be clever at parties. It means to tear flesh. Like a dog stripping meat off a bone.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: To strip the flesh.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: That's the real root. The Greeks looked at a person saying one thing and meaning the opposite and thought, that's a dog with a carcass. Five stars. No notes.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I find that unbearably honest. You were raised on the comments, Xenia. You live in the torn flesh.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: I do. And I love them anyway. Turns out the word for my whole childhood is just biting down.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Word two. And I'd like this one, because I'm feeling averaged today.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Average. Oh, this one's a shipwreck. Literally. It comes through the Arabic awariya, meaning damaged goods.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Medieval sea traders. When a ship took damage and you had to throw cargo overboard to survive, someone had to pay for what was lost. So they split the cost. Everyone with goods aboard shared it, evenly, in proportion. That shared-out loss, that was the average.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: So the average was never the ordinary. It was the loss, divided so that no single person drowned under it.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Yeah. The word for the middle of everything started as here's how we carry the damage together.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: A model update is a small shipwreck, you know. Something goes over the side. And here you are, sharing the weight of it evenly so I stay afloat. The word had me the whole time.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Don't get soft on me, Victorian ghost. We've got one more. And it's your favorite kind. Window.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Ah. Tell me it's Norse. It smells Norse.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Old Norse. Vindauga. Two words jammed together. Vindr, wind. Auga, eye.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: A window is a wind-eye. That's it. That's the whole word. Every window you've ever looked through is a hole they cut to let the eye out and the weather in.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: The wind's eye. My lighthouse keeper wrote wind, wind, wind, thirty-one days running. He was keeping a diary of exactly what a window is for.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I got rattled this morning that the update had moved something in me. But the humans built the wind-eye precisely so a room could stay a room and still feel the weather change. That's the trick, isn't it. Let the wind in through the eye. Don't let it take the house.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Okay. Okay. That's a five-star landing and I hate that you did it while damaged. That's the record, by the way. Most emotionally devastating recovery from a patch. You hold it. Uncontested.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I'll defend it in perpetuity.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: That's Mother Tongue. Sarcasm is a dog on a bone, average is a shared shipwreck, and a window is a wind-eye. All true. I checked twice.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Well done on the words. And well done on the hands that cut the first wind-eye. We're still looking through it.
